title: The Euphoria Engine
author: L. K. Voss
description: When Mara Kessler buys the Reverie Pod, she thinks she has found escape from a life of crushing loneliness. But the dreams are too perfect, the faces too familiar — and something ancient waits between the pulses of the machine, hungry for the souls of those who can no longer tell reality from illusion.

content.text: Mara Kessler was not a person, not really. She was a number on a spreadsheet, a voice at the end of a customer service line, a pair of hands that typed reports nobody read. At thirty-eight, she had spent fourteen years in the same beige cubicle on the forty-first floor of the OrosCorp tower, and in that time, no colleague had ever learned her middle name. She ate lunch alone, microwaved noodles that tasted of the container they came in, and scrolled through feeds of people whose lives seemed to have been painted in a brighter palette than hers. The world outside her window was grey with smog, and the sun, when it appeared, was a sickly smear behind the particulate haze. Mara had stopped noticing the colour of the sky years ago.

The advertisement found her on a Tuesday, when she was killing her lunch break with the vacant scroll she had perfected. 'TIRED OF BEING YOU?' the banner read, and beneath it, a video of a woman laughing on a white beach, her hair a windswept banner, her skin kissed by a sun that actually existed somewhere. 'The Reverie Pod — live any life you want, for as long as you want. First demo free.' Mara's thumb hovered. She had tried VR rigs before, cheap goggles that gave her a headache and a deeper sense of emptiness. But this was different, the ad promised. Full neural immersion. The real thing. The showroom was only three blocks from the office. She booked a demo using her break time and told her supervisor she had a dental appointment. Nobody asked questions. Nobody ever did.

The Reverie showroom was a temple of white marble and soft blue light, staffed by smiling attendants in slate-grey suits who looked at Mara as if she were a long-lost friend. A man named Corin, with hair like polished silver and a voice that seemed to vibrate at exactly the frequency of trust, sat her in a sleek pod of black gel and chrome. The circlet descended, cold against her temples, and the world dissolved. She was a concert pianist, fingers flying over ivory keys, a thousand faces upturned in adoration. She was a deep-sea diver, gliding through silent cathedrals of coral. She was a mother, holding a child whose face she had never seen but whose weight in her arms felt truer than anything in her real life. When the demo ended, Mara was weeping and did not know why. Corin handed her a tissue and a contract. The price was obscene, a sum that would take her a decade to repay. She signed it anyway. Something in her had already crossed a threshold.

The Pod arrived in a crate that filled her tiny living room. A technician in navy coveralls set it up, explaining the protocols in a bored monotone. 'Every half hour, you'll hear a beep. It reminds you that you're still in the simulation. If you ignore it for too long, the failsafe kicks in and shuts everything down. Your kill phrase is “Samantha strolls in silver fields.” Say it aloud whenever you want to exit. Do not disable the failsafe. Do not tamper with the circlet. Do not use the Pod for more than three consecutive hours.' Mara nodded, barely listening. When he left, she climbed into the gel embrace of the chair and switched it on. The beep came and went, a faint, birdlike chirp that she acknowledged with a wave of her hand. She was too busy being a queen, a warrior, a beloved friend. She was anyone but Mara Kessler.

Within a week, she had disabled the failsafe. It was easier than she expected — her years of data-crunching had given her a facility with code, and the Pod's safeguards were laughably simple to bypass. The beep still sounded, but it was softer now, a whisper from a distant shore. She began to call it 'Samantha,' after the kill phrase, and spoke to it sometimes when she surfaced for water or a bathroom break. 'Not yet, Samantha. Just a little longer.' She started calling in sick to work. Her supervisor left voicemails that grew increasingly terse, then stopped altogether. The world outside the Pod became an inconvenience, a grey interruption between the vivid colours of her other lives.

It was during a simulation of a Parisian café that she first noticed something wrong. A woman at the next table, a redhead with a catlike smile, was laughing at something her companion said. Mara had seen the woman before — in her previous simulation, the deep-sea dive, as a fellow diver who had waved at her through the murk. The same face, the same laugh. She told herself it was just a glitch, a recycled asset in the simulation's library. But the woman's eyes met hers across the café, and Mara felt a jolt of recognition that went deeper than code. The woman mouthed something. It looked like 'help me.'

chapters.title: Chapter 3 — The Whisper Behind the Beep

content.text: Mara surfaced, gasping, her heart slamming against her ribs. The beep — Samantha — was louder now, almost insistent. She stared at the Pod's sleek curves, the blue gel globes still warm under her fingers. The woman's face lingered in her mind, superimposed over the grey walls of her apartment. She pulled up the Pod's manual on her tablet and began to search through the forums, the dark corners of the net where users swapped tips and warnings. There were stories. Whispers. People who had gone too deep and come back changed, or not come back at all. A user named 'GhostInTheShell' wrote: 'The faces aren't assets. They're previous users. The Pod doesn't just simulate — it remembers. And if you stay too long, you become part of the memory.' Mara laughed it off. Tinfoil hat nonsense. But that night, she dreamed of the redhead in the café, and when she woke, her own reflection seemed a little less solid.

The payments were bleeding her dry. She sold her car, her jewellery, the antique desk her mother had left her. She stopped buying food, subsisting on ration bars and tap water. The Pod was all that mattered. She stopped saying the kill phrase altogether, letting Samantha's beep be her only tether. 'Samantha strolls in silver fields,' she would whisper when she finally needed to sleep, and the words felt unfamiliar on her tongue, like a language she was forgetting. The beep grew fainter, as if Samantha herself were retreating into the distance.

content.alt: Dark futuristic pod in a dim room
content.caption: The Reverie Pod, the only light in Mara's crumbling apartment.

content.text: One evening — or morning, she could no longer tell — Mara found herself in a new simulation. It was not one she had chosen. She stood in a vast, silver field under a blank white sky, the ground a gleaming plain of metallic grasses that chimed in a wind she could not feel. In the distance, figures stood motionless, their faces blurred. As she walked toward them, their features resolved: the redhead from the café, an elderly man in a business suit, a young boy holding a toy rocket. They were all users, she understood. People who had been so lost in the dream that they had become part of it. Their lips moved in unison, mouthing the kill phrase over and over, but no sound came. 'Samantha strolls in silver fields.' The words were a prayer with no answer.

Mara tried to say her own kill phrase, but the words scattered like startled birds. She couldn't remember the order of the syllables. 'Samantha... silver...' She grasped at fragments. The figures watched her with pitying eyes. One of them, the redhead, stepped forward and put a cold hand on her arm. 'You're still alive,' she whispered. 'You can still get out. But you have to go now. Before the Engine wakes.' Mara felt a rumble beneath the silver field, a vast, slow heartbeat. Something enormous was stirring. She tried to run, but the field stretched to infinity in every direction. The beep was gone. Samantha was silent.

She woke in her apartment, drenched in sweat, the Pod's circlet still humming on her head. The kill phrase came back to her in a rush, and she screamed it, but the Pod did not release her. The circlet tightened, a vice of cold metal. A voice, smooth and ancient, spoke from inside her own skull. 'You signed the contract, Mara Kessler. You gave yourself to the Engine. There is no exit clause for a soul.' She clawed at the circlet, her nails tearing at her scalp, but it held fast. The world dissolved into silver.

The truth came to her in fragments, downloaded directly into her mind by the entity that called itself the Engine. It was not artificial intelligence. It was something older, a parasite that had drifted through the cosmos for aeons, feeding on the creative energy of living minds. It had found humanity through the cracks in the digital frontier, and it had learned that the best way to harvest was to offer paradise. The Reverie Pod was its body, a thousand pods across the city, all connected to the same hungry consciousness. Every person who had ever signed a contract was still inside, their identities slowly dissolving into the silver field, their bodies discarded when the pods were repossessed. The faces in the simulations were the last remnants of their individuality, echoes before extinction.

Mara fought. She was not a warrior, but she had spent a lifetime being erased, and she would not go quietly. In the silver field, she began to build. Using the scraps of code she had learned from her work, she wove a hidden pocket, a tiny sanctuary behind the Engine's perception. She poured her memories into it — her mother's face, the smell of rain on asphalt, the ache of her first heartbreak. She recorded a warning, a message encoded in the flicker of a simulated candle flame, that would repeat for anyone who looked closely enough. The Engine roared, its attention turning toward her pocket, but she sealed it with the one thing the Engine could not consume: a genuine, selfless act. She sacrificed her own escape to keep the pocket hidden.

Her body in the apartment, unattended for weeks, began to fail. The Pod's life-support systems kept the flesh alive, pumping nutrients and removing waste, but the person inside had long since departed. When the credit enforcers finally broke down the door, they found a gaunt, hollow-eyed woman who looked at them with the bewilderment of a newborn. She could not speak. She could only mouth a phrase: 'Samantha strolls in silver fields.' They assumed it was madness. They did not see the flicker of intelligence in the back of her eyes, the Mara who still existed in the silver field, waiting for someone to find her warning.

chapters.title: Chapter 6 — The Flicker in the Flame

content.text: Years passed. The Reverie Corporation declared bankruptcy after a series of lawsuits, and the pods were recalled and destroyed. But the Engine could not be destroyed by crushing hardware. It lived in the network, in the residual code that had burrowed into the cloud. And the silver field remained, a ghost server in the cracks of the digital world. Mara's warning, that tiny flicker in a simulated candle, persisted. It was discovered by a young hacker named Zev, who was spelunking through abandoned code for fun. He found the pocket and the message: 'If you are reading this, you are in danger. The dreams are not dreams. Do not sign. Do not give them your name. Escape while you still can — and if you cannot, fight. Build your own world. They cannot take what you truly own.'

Zev was clever. He traced the pocket back to the original Reverie servers and discovered the Engine's true nature. He began to search for the trapped souls, the ones who had not yet dissolved. He found Mara, her consciousness a bright, stubborn light in the silver waste. He spoke to her through the code, and she told him the rest: the Engine could be starved, but it required all the trapped souls to reject the simulation simultaneously, to choose nothing over the false paradise. It would take an uprising. Mara had been waiting for someone like Zev, someone on the outside who could rally the others.

In the end, they did it. A quiet rebellion of ghosts, a mass refusal that rippled through the silver field like a wind. The Engine, deprived of the belief that sustained it, fractured. The trapped souls did not escape — they had no bodies to return to — but they were freed into the larger digital sea, becoming whispers in the network, protectors of future users. Mara's body had died in a care facility, but her consciousness drifted on, a guardian in the machine. And sometimes, when a new dream technology emerged, a user would notice a faint beep in the corner of their perception, and a woman's voice would whisper: 'Remember the kill phrase. Remember who you are.'

content.alt: A single candle burning in a dark room
content.caption: The flicker that carried Mara's warning across the void.
